channel.title: Latest alerts from washingtonpost.com
channel.description: An unofficial feed created by Derek Willis.

item.title: Trump showed classified map to passengers on his private plane in 2022, memo says
item.description: The document offers a snapshot of an early moment in special counsel Jack Smith’s investigation and adds new shading to the public understanding of Smith’s probes.

item.title: U.S. has 15-point plan to end war with Iran, including demand to remove all enriched uranium, officials say
item.description: The proposal offered sanctions relief to Iran in return for U.S. demands, the officials said. A person familiar with the plan said it was delivered to Iran.

item.title: A young senator in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Tuesday points.

item.title: She taught backyard swim lessons. One complaint shut them down.
item.description: Local governments should encourage home businesses instead of making them illegal.

item.title: In Trump’s war messaging, veterans see something new — and disturbing
item.description: Service members and families who lost loved ones say the Trump team’s memes and jokes trivialize combat and sacrifice. Trump aides say the backlash sends views soaring.

item.title: War memes. Election upset. Minnesota shootings. Justice Department. Meta fine. Meningitis. Improving with age. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Pentagon orders Army paratroopers to the Middle East as U.S. weighs a significant escalation in the war with Iran
item.description: U.S. officials approved written orders for a couple thousand soldiers from the 82nd Airborne Division’s 1st Brigade Combat Team and its headquarters at Fort Bragg in North Carolina, people familiar with the move said. Many of the soldiers are part of the division’s Immediate Response Force, a unit that is trained to deploy on 18 hours’ notice for missions as varied as seizing airfields and enabling emergency evacuations.

item.title: Florida Democrat wins special election for seat in state House district that includes Trump’s Mar-a-Lago estate
item.description: The stunning upset by first-time candidate Emily Gregory signals significant Democratic momentum ahead of the 2026 midterms and is the latest in a string of overperformances by the party in elections since the start of Trump’s second term. She defeated Jon Maples, a Republican endorsed by the president who ran in line with Trump’s policies.

item.title: Exercise can lower Alzheimer’s risk. Scientists may have discovered why.
item.description: In an ambitious study, memory and learning abilities improved substantially after exercise among mice with a form of the disease. The authors found that during and after exercise, the liver in mice releases a specialized protein that travels to the brain and helps repair the blood-brain barrier, which may improve brain health and potentially fight dementia.

item.title: Audio from cockpit recorder reveals error before deadly LaGuardia crash
item.description: LaGuardia’s air traffic control tower failed to recognize that it had granted permission for a plane and an emergency vehicle to use the same runway, officials said.

item.title: Minnesota sues Trump administration over withheld evidence in shootings by federal officers
item.description: State officials allege the federal government is trying to evade responsibility for shootings by officers amid an immigration crackdown that killed two citizens and wounded an undocumented immigrant.

item.title: Here’s what travelers need to know about ICE officers in airports
item.description: Agents arrive amid TSA staffing shortages and hours-long security lines.

item.title: Prosecutor admits government lacks evidence of misconduct by Fed Chair Jerome H. Powell
item.description: The admission during a closed-door hearing undercuts President Donald Trump’s claims of “criminality” in the central bank’s $2.5 billion office renovations.

item.title: Senate Republicans and White House discussing potential deal to end partial shutdown of Department of Homeland Security
item.description: The potential agreement, which Democrats have not signed off on, would reopen DHS, which has been partially shut down since Feb. 14.

item.title: Nobody needs over $100,000 per year in Social Security benefits
item.description: Capping benefits for the wealthiest seniors is a good starting point for reforming the program.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Mom-in-law’s mental decline strains visits to breaking point
item.description: After a stroke, mother-in-law loses her filter, and her offended family members struggle to adjust.

item.title: Oklahoma energy executive Alan S. Armstrong to temporarily fill Markwayne Mullin’s Senate seat
item.description: The Oklahoma governor said he will name the executive chairman of Williams, a natural gas company, to the Senate seat vacated by Mullin (R), who was recently confirmed as homeland security secretary.

item.title: Iran officials deny negotiations with U.S. after Trump says peace talks are progressing
item.description: Egypt, Pakistan and Turkey have taken the lead in efforts to broker a peace deal between the United States and Iran, though conversations have been indirect thus far.

item.title: A White House statue in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Tuesday points.

item.title: Iran talks. LaGuardia crash. Mail-in ballots. ICE at airports. AI nurses. Unusual wildfires. Alzheimer’s study. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Senate votes 54 to 45 to confirm Markwayne Mullin as Homeland Security secretary
item.description: The Oklahoma Republican portrayed himself as an experienced lawmaker ready to restore DHS’s credibility after a surge in immigration enforcement resulted in the killing of two U.S. citizens.

item.title: Pentagon to move journalists to external site after court loss over restricted access
item.description: A Pentagon spokesman said the Defense Department cannot adequately manage security within the five-sided building with working press inside. He announced that the department has closed the “Correspondents’ Corridor” where journalists have worked for decades and will move them to an “annex facility outside the Pentagon, but still on Pentagon grounds.” Journalists will now require an escort by authorized Pentagon staffers to enter the building outside of scheduled press briefings and interviews, the spokesman added.

item.title: In tense moments after deadly LaGuardia crash, controller said he ‘messed up’
item.description: Officials defended the airport’s air traffic control staffing after the collision, which killed two pilots and hospitalized dozens of passengers.

item.title: Republicans, publicly bullish about Senate seat in Georgia, fret about incumbent Democrat Jon Ossoff
item.description: Behind closed doors, Republicans have tamped down their hopes of unseating Jon Ossoff, a 39-year-old powerhouse fundraiser, as he seeks another term.

item.title: D.C.’s cherry blossom buds reach Stage 5 of 6, one away from peak bloom
item.description: The National Park Service said Monday that the blossom buds reached their fifth stage, puffy white, which is one away from peak bloom. At this stage, the buds swell and the blossoms are on the brink of bursting out.

item.title: Supreme Court appears ready to limit mail-in balloting ahead of midterms
item.description: The Supreme Court on Monday appeared likely to embrace a conservative challenge to tallying mail-in ballots that arrive after Election Day, a move that could upend election procedures in states across the country as voters prepare to cast ballots in the midterm elections.

item.title: Trump administration to pay $1 billion to stop two East Coast wind farms
item.description: The Interior Department reached a settlement to pay France’s TotalEnergies not to pursue two offshore wind farms but direct the investments toward fossil fuels.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Wife seethes at his lack of boundaries with clingy friend
item.description: As wife presses the letter writer to set boundaries with a clingy friend — and others — he struggles with loss of self.

item.title: There’s a dark, disruptive symbolism in Trump’s plans to renovate Washington, our columnist writes
item.description: The president’s garish and piecemeal changes to the capital city’s urban design are poised to destroy symbolism upheld for centuries, Philip Kennicott writes.

item.title: So long, farewell, Kennedy Center. Let’s put on a happy face.
item.description: With the upcoming Kennedy Center closure, D.C. nightlife needs a cultural infusion.

item.title: A season is canceled in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Monday points.

item.title: Trump says U.S. is postponing strikes as it negotiates end to war with Iran
item.description: President Trump said he had told the U.S. military to postpone strikes against Iranian energy infrastructure for five days, while talks proceed.

item.title: Energy prices. ICE at airports. LaGuardia collision. Columbus statue. Casey Means. Cancer risks. Aging mistakes. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Trump threats, U.S. troop build-up raise specter of battle for Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Israeli officials said securing the strait for energy shipments could become the war’s main goal now that regime change and ending Iran’s ability to obtain a nuclear weapon seem out of reach.

item.title: Trump installs Christopher Columbus statue on White House grounds
item.description: The installation marks President Donald Trump's latest effort to remake the White House campus. The piece is a reconstruction of a statue that protesters dumped into Baltimore's harbor in 2020.

item.title: A luau’s last night shows Hawaii’s changing tourist tastes
item.description: Paradise Cove entertained thousands of guests and employed generations of locals over the decades. But the nature of tourism in the state is changing.

item.title: Four truths about covid that have become clouded over time
item.description: In the early days, the virus posed a graver threat to people and the health care system, President Donald Trump embraced lockdowns he now blasts, and the benefits of vaccines were oversold.

item.title: Why Iran does not appear ready to give in, despite heavy losses
item.description: Tehran’s unwillingness to capitulate as the war enters its fourth week is wrapped up in the power it exerts over the Strait of Hormuz, officials in the region say.

item.title: As Trump says ICE will deploy to airports Monday, border czar says he is working to identify what agents will do and where
item.description: A month into the government funding standoff that is straining airports, Tom Homan said Sunday that ICE agents "will be at the airports tomorrow helping TSA move those lines along" but will not be involved in X-ray screening or other work that requires specialized training.

item.title: Still need to finish this weekend’s On the Record? We’ve got you.
item.description: Play now to finish out your score for the week.

item.title: One gas station, two drivers, three fill-ups: The Iran war hits this state especially hard
item.description: In Colorado, gasoline prices that keep climbing have changed daily lives for many. But the effects are not equally felt.

item.title: I want to avoid the ‘aging mistakes’ my parents made. I’ve got work to do, our columnist writes.
item.description: Twenty years ago, contributor Steven Petrow started a list of what he considered “stupid things” that old people do. Now, he’s seeing his life, and his list, in a different light.

item.title: Crossword and a coffee: Solve the Sunday puzzle now
item.description: Crossword and a coffee: Solve the Sunday puzzle now

item.title: Trump threatens to strike Iran’s power plants if the country does not fully open the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours
item.description: In a Saturday night post on Truth Social, the president said the U.S. would “obliterate” Iran’s power plants if it did not fully open the waterway “without threat … from this exact point in time.” Energy prices have continued to spike during the war, with Tehran having largely shut down the vital shipping chokepoint.

item.title: Column: Congress could be headed for a tipping point and an irreversible decline
item.description: Critics are sounding the alarm that the institution needs a major course correction or else it will slide into a permanent state of weakness that further empowers the presidency, columnist Paul Kane writes.

item.title: A massive border wall expansion is underway. Pushback is coming from all sides.
item.description: The aggressive pace of the project has alarmed advocates who say the construction will destroy pristine country, threaten endangered species, and cut off access to sacred Indigenous and archaeological sites.

item.title: A teen’s traumatic path from bullying and abuse to Trump’s State of the Union
item.description: Sage Blair ran away — from bullies, from school officials who did not tell family of her gender transition, from parents who didn’t believe a person can change genders. Rape, a lawsuit and an invitation from the president followed.

item.title: Trump threatens to deploy ICE to airports as TSA shortages drive delays
item.description: Deploying agents to checkpoints nationwide would mark an unprecedented expansion of immigration enforcement even as Democrats push for tighter limits on how those agents operate, citing concerns that the administration has fast-tracked training to expand ICE ranks.

item.title: Robert Mueller has died at 81. He became a political target as special counsel probing Russian interference in the 2016 election.
item.description: At the FBI, in an era of increasingly partisan national division, he built a reputation for nonpartisan rectitude and stone-faced reserve. Then, in his last major role on the national stage, Mr. Mueller found himself at the center of a political tempest.

item.title: To tilt Hungarian election, Russians proposed staging an assassination attempt
item.description: To aid Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, a friend of Russia, in his election, operatives proposed “the Gamechanger” — a staged assassination attempt to stir supporters.
